intro: Angle facts and parallel line rules are the building blocks of geometry. They allow you to find unknown angles in diagrams without measuring — using logic and known properties.
explanation:
Basic angle facts:
- Angles on a straight line add up to 180 degrees.
- Angles around a point add up to 360 degrees.
- Vertically opposite angles are equal (formed by two crossing lines).

Angles in shapes:
- Angles in a triangle add up to 180 degrees.
- Angles in a quadrilateral add up to 360 degrees.
- Interior angles of a regular polygon: (n - 2) x 180 / n, where n is the number of sides.
- Exterior angles of any polygon add up to 360 degrees.

Parallel line angle rules:
When a straight line (transversal) crosses two parallel lines:
- Alternate angles are equal (Z-angles). They are on opposite sides of the transversal.
- Corresponding angles are equal (F-angles). They are in matching positions on each parallel line.
- Co-interior (allied) angles add up to 180 degrees (C-angles). They are on the same side of the transversal, between the parallel lines.

You must name the rule you use when giving reasons in geometry proofs.

worked_example.question: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One angle formed is 65 degrees. Find the alternate angle, the corresponding angle, and the co-interior angle.
steps: Alternate angle = 65 degrees (alternate angles are equal — Z-angle).
Corresponding angle = 65 degrees (corresponding angles are equal — F-angle).
Co-interior angle = 180 - 65 = 115 degrees (co-interior angles add up to 180 degrees — C-angle).
worked_example.final_answer: Alternate: 65 degrees, Corresponding: 65 degrees, Co-interior: 115 degrees

common_mistakes: Confusing alternate and co-interior angles — alternate angles are equal, co-interior angles add up to 180 degrees.
Not giving reasons when asked — angle problems in exams often require you to state the rule (e.g. 'alternate angles in parallel lines').
Assuming lines are parallel without it being stated — only use parallel line rules when parallel lines are clearly indicated with arrows.
